statement: Indo-Portuguese palette is shared across the whole house: Mangalore-tile floor, lime-plaster walls, exposed rosewood beams over a terracotta-tile underside ceiling, oyster-shell windows, slow ceiling fan, late-afternoon warm light. Every interior must read as the same single house.

This room is a single open-plan volume with NO interior partition walls. Three functional zones coexist within one ~56 m^2 great-room: living (east half), dining (north-west quadrant), kitchen (south-west quadrant). Override any 'dining-dominant' interpretation: the three zones share the room equally and at least two of them must be visible in any framing.

Living zone (east half, against the bedroom-side wall): a low rosewood coffee table on a coir or jute rug, two cane-seat planter chairs, a carved rosewood settee with embroidered cushions facing west toward the dining table, a console with a brass lamp.

Dining zone (north-west quadrant, near the balcao door): a long rosewood dining table with a runner and six cane-back chairs, a brass-and-glass pendant lamp directly over the table, a sideboard with porcelain plates against the north wall.

Kitchen zone (south-west quadrant, against the utility wall): a built-in masonry counter with a black-stone top along the south wall, an iron stove, a stone sink under the west window, copper and brass utensils on open rosewood shelves, terracotta jars on the floor, and a freestanding kitchen island with a stone top anchoring the zone.

The room is double-height: the ceiling rises to the underside of the upper floor, exposing rosewood beams under terracotta tile. The upper-floor corridor balustrade is visible above (turned-baluster railing along the south edge of the upper void) — a person on the upper corridor would look DOWN through an open balustrade into this room.

The south wall is a continuous solid plastered wall — the staircase is enclosed in a separate stairwell behind that wall and is NOT visible inside this room. Show a single closed timber-panel door on the south wall (leading to the stairwell) and otherwise unbroken lime-plaster wall. Do NOT render any stair flight, stair treads, balusters, or stairwell visible inside this room.

The balcao opens directly onto a tropical garden: through the wide arches the camera must see lush garden foliage, the trunk and canopy of a mature mango tree dominating the view, beds of tulsi and bougainvillea, and a stone-paved path heading away. Treat the openings as fully open arches, not glazed windows.

The utility room is equipped with modern appliances integrated into the masonry counter or stacked along one wall: a built-in dishwasher (stainless steel front, fitted under the counter), a front-load washing machine, and a separate front-load dryer (stacked or side-by-side with the washing machine). Show all three appliances clearly. Keep the rest of the room traditional Goan service-room (terracotta tile, lime plaster, hanging linen, laundry sink), but the appliances must read as 21st-century white-goods, not vintage.
